title: QuickBooks Online Custom Reports Are Moving to Modern View (June 2026): What Firms Must Do Now
excerpt: Intuit is migrating QBO custom reports to modern view in company batches from June 2026. What breaks, and a 5-step checklist for firms.

mainEntity.name: When exactly will my clients' custom reports move to modern view?

acceptedAnswer.text: Per Intuit's May 2026 product update, custom reports built in classic view migrate to modern view in batches, by company, starting June 2026. Different client files migrate on different dates rather than all at once. Between June 15 and August 15, 2026, migrated custom reports default to modern view with a temporary option to switch back to classic; on August 15, 2026, that option is removed permanently. Rollout details can change, so check Intuit's official product updates page for the current schedule.

mainEntity.name: Will my saved customizations, filters, and scheduled report emails carry over?

acceptedAnswer.text: Intuit states that report data, filters, favorites, report schedules, and customizations carry over automatically and won't need to be recreated. In practice, treat that as the goal rather than a guarantee: modern view lays out columns, groupings, and formatting differently, so a faithfully migrated report can still render differently in exports and scheduled emails. Screenshot settings and export a reference copy of every mission-critical report before each client's file migrates, then verify the first outputs after migration.

mainEntity.name: Can I keep using classic view reports after the migration?

acceptedAnswer.text: Only briefly. After June 15, 2026, classic view is no longer available for standard reports across QuickBooks Online, Intuit Accountant Suite, and Intuit Enterprise Suite. For custom reports, a switch-back-to-classic option remains during the transition window, but Intuit has said it will be removed permanently on August 15, 2026. After that, modern view is the only reporting experience.

mainEntity.name: How should a firm with dozens of QuickBooks clients prepare for the batch-by-company rollout?

acceptedAnswer.text: Five steps: inventory the custom reports you actually use in each client file; screenshot and export the definitions of mission-critical ones; list every scheduled report and flag client-facing schedules for post-migration verification; treat your first migrated clients as a test group and turn what you learn into an internal playbook; and email clients before their files migrate so the change is expected. Longer term, move firm-critical deliverables to a reporting layer defined outside QuickBooks, such as FinBoard.ai, so vendor UI migrations stop putting your client packets at risk.

content:
QuickBooks Online Custom Reports Are Moving to Modern View (June 2026): What Firms Must Do Now

Starting June 2026, Intuit began migrating custom reports built in QuickBooks Online's classic view over to the new modern view. The migration happens in batches, company by company. If you're an accounting firm managing dozens of client files, that one detail changes everything: your clients will not all move at once. One client's custom reports may look completely different this week while another's stay untouched until later in the summer. Per Intuit's May 2026 product update, after June 15, 2026, the classic view for the 150+ standard reports is no longer available across QuickBooks Online, the Intuit Accountant Suite, and Intuit Enterprise Suite. Between June 15 and August 15, 2026, custom reports default to modern view with a temporary option to switch back to classic. On August 15, that option is removed permanently. If your month-end packets, board reports, or scheduled client emails depend on saved custom reports, the window to prepare is now.

What's Changing, and When

QuickBooks Online has run two reporting experiences in parallel for years: the long-standing classic view and the newer modern view, which adds flexible columns, new grouping and pivot options, in-report charts, and visual formatting. Standard reports moved first. Custom reports are the last piece, and they're moving now. Those saved definitions are the ones firms actually rely on for client deliverables.

Here is the timeline as Intuit has communicated it:

  • June 2026: Custom reports created in classic view begin migrating to modern view in batches, by company. Not every QuickBooks Online company file migrates on the same date.
  • After June 15, 2026: Classic view is no longer available for standard reports across QuickBooks Online, Intuit Accountant Suite, and Intuit Enterprise Suite.
  • June 15 to August 15, 2026: Migrated custom reports default to modern view, but a temporary "switch to classic view" escape hatch remains for custom reports.
  • August 15, 2026: The option to switch back to classic view is removed permanently. Modern view becomes the only reporting experience.

Intuit says report data, filters, favorites, report schedules, and customizations will carry over automatically, with no need to recreate reports manually. That's the plan on paper. But any migration touching thousands of saved report definitions across millions of company files will have edge cases, and Intuit's rollout details have shifted before. Treat the dates above as the current published plan, and check Intuit's official QuickBooks Online product updates page for the latest before you rely on any specific cutoff.

Who Is Affected: Firms With Many Client Files Face a Staggered Migration

A single-company business owner will experience this as a one-time UI change. Accounting firms will not. Because the migration rolls out in company-specific batches, a firm managing 30, 50, or 100 client files will live through a stretch, potentially weeks long, where some clients are on modern view custom reports and others are still on classic. That creates real operational problems:

  • Inconsistent workflows across staff. A staff accountant running the same monthly close checklist for two clients may face two different reporting interfaces, with customization options in different places and layouts that render differently.
  • No single "migration day" to plan around. You can't block out one weekend to verify everything. Verification has to happen client by client, as each company's batch lands.
  • Client-facing output changes without warning. If a client opens their own QuickBooks file and their familiar custom report suddenly looks different, your firm gets the confused email, whether or not you knew their batch had migrated.
  • Documentation drift. Internal SOPs with screenshots of the classic customization panel become wrong for some clients and right for others, at the same time.

Firms that deliver standardized monthly reporting packets across their whole client base lean hardest on saved custom reports, and they have the most surface area exposed to this change. If that's you, we've written before about how firms deliver consolidated reports across 50 QuickBooks clients, and the operational discipline described there is exactly what this migration will stress-test.

What Can Break (Even If Intuit Says It Won't)

Intuit's stated position is that customizations, filters, schedules, and favorites carry over automatically. Take that as the goal, not a guarantee, and pay attention to the categories with the highest breakage risk:

  • Saved customizations and layouts. Modern view organizes columns, groupings, and filters differently than classic view. A report that carries its data over faithfully can still render with different column widths, grouping order, or header behavior, which matters when the output goes straight into a client deliverable.
  • Scheduled report emails. Report schedules are supposed to migrate. But a schedule that fires from a migrated report definition may produce output formatted differently than what clients have received for years. The email arrives on time; the attachment looks wrong. Nobody notices until a client asks.
  • Memorized report structures built years ago. Old custom reports built on legacy customization options are the most likely edge cases in any automated migration. The older and more heavily customized the report, the more carefully it should be checked after its company migrates.
  • Client-facing report packets. If your monthly packet is assembled by exporting several custom reports to Excel or PDF and combining them, a formatting change in any one report changes the packet. Multiply that by every client on a different migration date.
  • Exports and downstream spreadsheets. Spreadsheets that expect exported data in specific cells or column positions can silently misread a re-shaped export. Silent is the operative word. The formula still computes, just on the wrong cells.

The 5-Step Preparation Checklist

You can't control when each client's batch migrates. You can control how prepared you are when it does.

  1. Inventory custom reports per client, starting now. For every client file, list the custom reports the firm actually uses: name, purpose, who consumes it, and whether it feeds a scheduled email or a monthly packet. Most firms discover they rely on far fewer reports than exist in the file, and that a handful are genuinely mission-critical. The inventory tells you where to focus.
  2. Screenshot and export report definitions. Before migration hits a client, capture what "correct" looks like: screenshot the customization settings (filters, columns, date logic, grouping) and export a current run of each critical report to Excel or PDF. If a migrated report renders differently, you'll have an authoritative reference to rebuild from instead of reconstructing settings from memory.
  3. Identify mission-critical scheduled reports. Scheduled emails are the reports that break invisibly. List every report schedule across every client file, note the recipients, and flag the ones going directly to clients, lenders, or boards. Those are the ones to verify manually after each company migrates. Open the next scheduled output and compare it against your saved reference.
  4. Test in migrated companies first, then apply the lessons everywhere. The batch-by-company rollout has one upside: your first migrated clients are a free preview. When a client's file moves to modern view, have one person run through that client's critical reports, document what changed and what needed fixing, and turn it into a short internal playbook. Every subsequent client migration then takes minutes instead of an afternoon of rediscovery.
  5. Communicate to clients before they notice. A two-paragraph email telling clients that QuickBooks is updating its reporting interface between June and August, that their reports may look slightly different, and that you're verifying everything as the change reaches their file costs almost nothing. It converts a confusing surprise into evidence that your firm is on top of things. Firms that stay silent absorb the confused calls one by one.

The Bigger Lesson: Firm-Critical Reporting Shouldn't Live Inside a Vendor's UI Migration Path

Step back from the June 2026 dates and there's a structural point worth sitting with. The saved customizations, the schedules, the layouts your clients recognize: all of it is exposed because it lives inside QuickBooks Online's interface. When the vendor changes the interface, your deliverables change with it, on the vendor's timeline, not yours.

This isn't a criticism of Intuit. Every platform eventually retires old surfaces. It's an argument about architecture. QuickBooks Online is an excellent system of record: it holds the general ledger, the transactions, the chart of accounts. But a firm's reporting layer is firm intellectual property: the definitions of what a monthly packet contains, how a consolidated P&L is structured, which KPIs a client's board sees. When that IP is stored as saved settings inside a vendor's UI, it is only as stable as the vendor's product roadmap. This migration is a mild version of the risk. The severe version is a future change that doesn't come with a carry-over promise at all.

The resilient pattern is a clean separation: the ledger lives in QuickBooks; the reporting layer lives outside it. Report definitions, layouts, KPI formulas, and delivery schedules are maintained independently, pulling data from QuickBooks via API. When Intuit redesigns its report UI, nothing in your deliverables moves, because your deliverables were never built out of that UI in the first place. Teams evaluating this kind of architecture usually end up comparing a few platforms; our guide to the top financial software alternatives for modern finance teams covers the landscape.

How FinBoard.ai Gives You Migration-Proof Reports

FinBoard.ai is built on exactly that separation, which is why the modern view migration is a non-event for reports that run on it:

  • Reports are defined outside QuickBooks. Your P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, consolidated statements, and custom report structures are configurations in FinBoard, not saved settings in the QuickBooks UI. Intuit can redesign its reporting experience top to bottom and your report definitions don't move.
  • Data syncs from QuickBooks at the ledger level. FinBoard pulls transaction-level data through the QuickBooks Online API, then reconciles its outputs against QuickBooks' own numbers so you can prove the figures match the source of truth. UI migrations don't touch the API-level general ledger data your reports are built from.
  • Version-stable, multi-entity by design. One report structure applies across every client entity, so a 50-client firm maintains one consolidated reporting layer instead of 50 sets of per-file saved reports. Those per-file reports are exactly the asset this migration puts at risk. Report layouts stay identical month over month unless you change them, and every change is audited: who changed what, and when.
  • Client deliverables keep their format. Scheduled reports, dashboards, and exported packets are generated from FinBoard's definitions, so the output your clients receive in July looks like the output they received in May, regardless of which migration batch their QuickBooks file landed in.

The practical move for firms right now is to do both: run the five-step checklist above to protect your existing in-QBO reports through the migration window, and use this moment to move your genuinely firm-critical deliverables onto an independent reporting layer so the next vendor migration costs you nothing.

Frequently Asked Questions

When exactly will my clients' custom reports move to modern view?

Per Intuit's May 2026 product update, custom reports built in classic view migrate to modern view in batches, by company, starting June 2026. Different client files migrate on different dates rather than all at once. Between June 15 and August 15, 2026, migrated custom reports default to modern view with a temporary option to switch back to classic; on August 15, 2026, that option is removed permanently. Rollout details can change, so check Intuit's official product updates page for the current schedule.

Will my saved customizations, filters, and scheduled report emails carry over?

Intuit states that report data, filters, favorites, report schedules, and customizations carry over automatically and won't need to be recreated. In practice, treat that as the goal rather than a guarantee: modern view lays out columns, groupings, and formatting differently, so a faithfully migrated report can still render differently in exports and scheduled emails. Screenshot settings and export a reference copy of every mission-critical report before each client's file migrates, then verify the first outputs after migration.

Can I keep using classic view reports after the migration?

Only briefly. After June 15, 2026, classic view is no longer available for standard reports across QuickBooks Online, Intuit Accountant Suite, and Intuit Enterprise Suite. For custom reports, a switch-back-to-classic option remains during the transition window, but Intuit has said it will be removed permanently on August 15, 2026. After that, modern view is the only reporting experience, so adapt your workflows now rather than relying on the temporary escape hatch.

How should a firm with dozens of QuickBooks clients prepare for the batch-by-company rollout?

Five steps: inventory the custom reports you actually use in each client file; screenshot and export the definitions of mission-critical ones; list every scheduled report and flag client-facing schedules for post-migration verification; treat your first migrated clients as a test group and turn what you learn into an internal playbook; and email clients before their files migrate so the change is expected. Longer term, move firm-critical deliverables to a reporting layer defined outside QuickBooks, such as FinBoard.ai, so vendor UI migrations stop putting your client packets at risk.

Keep Your Client Reports Stable, Whatever Intuit Migrates Next

The June 2026 modern view migration will pass, and most reports will probably carry over fine. But the firms that come through it cleanly will be the ones that inventoried, documented, and verified. And the firms that never worry about the next migration will be the ones whose reporting layer doesn't live inside anyone's UI. FinBoard.ai syncs your clients' QuickBooks data, keeps report definitions version-stable and outside the migration path, and reconciles every number back to QuickBooks so accuracy is provable. Try FinBoard.ai and make this the last vendor UI migration your reporting packet ever depends on.
